back: Scalability: the capacity to add or remove resources to handle load. Elasticity: automatically scaling resources up AND down in response to real-time demand, with no manual step.
Vertical (scale up/down): increasing or decreasing the size of a single resource. Horizontal (scale out/in): adding or removing instances of a resource.
CapEx: large upfront investment in owned physical infrastructure. OpEx: ongoing, pay-as-you-go spending. Cloud shifts spending from CapEx to OpEx.
Billing based on actual resource usage rather than a fixed fee — especially cost-effective for variable or unpredictable workloads.
Infrastructure as a Service. Provider manages physical hardware and virtualization; customer manages the OS, runtime, and applications. Example: Azure VMs.
Platform as a Service. Provider manages hardware, OS, and runtime; customer focuses only on code and data. Example: Azure App Service, Azure SQL Database.
Software as a Service. Provider manages the entire stack including the application; customer just uses the software. Example: Microsoft 365.
Defines which security/management tasks fall to the provider vs. the customer. Moving IaaS → PaaS → SaaS shifts more to the provider, but data, identities, and devices always stay with the customer.
Servers still run the code, but the provider manages them fully and bills based on execution rather than pre-allocated capacity. Example: Azure Functions.
Infrastructure owned and operated by a third-party provider, shared (with logical isolation) across many tenants. Lowest CapEx, highest scalability.
Infrastructure dedicated to a single organization — more control, but more management responsibility — whether hosted on-prem or by a third party.
Combines public cloud with private/on-premises infrastructure, integrated together. Common for compliance needs or gradual migration.
Using more than one public cloud provider (e.g. Azure + AWS), often to avoid vendor lock-in or add redundancy. Not the same as hybrid cloud.
Designing a system to remain operational and accessible for a high percentage of time, typically expressed as an SLA percentage.
The speed at which cloud resources can be provisioned, configured, and deployed — enabling faster experimentation and time-to-market.
A system's ability to keep operating despite a component failure, without major disruption.
The strategy and tooling used to restore operations after a major outage — e.g. Azure Site Recovery failing over to a secondary region.
≈ 8.7 hours per year.
≈ 52.6 minutes per year.
≈ 4.4 hours per year.
≈ 3.65 days per year.
Verify explicitly, use least-privilege access, assume breach.
Physical → Identity & access → Perimeter → Network → Compute → Application → Data (outermost to innermost).
Fairness, reliability & safety, privacy & security, inclusiveness, transparency, accountability.
Renting compute, storage, networking, and software from a provider over the internet, paying for what you use, instead of buying and running your own hardware.

front: Why cloud shifts CapEx to OpEx
back: You stop buying and depreciating physical servers and instead pay ongoing costs tied to actual consumption, improving cash-flow flexibility.
The customer patches and manages the OS and everything installed on it; the provider manages the physical infrastructure and virtualization layer.
The customer manages only their application code and data; the provider manages OS patching and the runtime.
The customer manages their data, users, and device access; the provider manages literally everything else, including the application.

front: What never transfers to the provider
back: Data, identities, devices, and access management — these remain the customer's responsibility in every service model.
Using on-prem infrastructure for steady-state load and bursting into the public cloud during demand spikes — a hybrid cloud pattern.

front: Why 'serverless' is a bit of a misnomer
back: Servers still exist and run the code — 'serverless' just means the customer has zero visibility into or responsibility for managing them.
